Abstract is clear and well-structured with concrete claims (30-40% error reduction)
limitations: Core method—exponential smoother with quality-adaptive gain—is incremental; EMA variants with SNR-weighted gains are well-known in tracking literature
Evaluation relies entirely on simulations; no real-world TDoA measurements, no hardware-in-the-loop, no field trials with actual 5G-NR gNBs
No theoretical analysis provided (e.g., CRLB comparison, convergence guarantees, optimality bounds); claims rest on empirical simulation only
Baselines are unclear—30-40% improvement over what? Raw measurements? Kalman filter? Other denoisers?
UAV-specific channel dynamics (3D mobility, NLOS transitions, altitude-dependent multipath) not evidently addressed
quality_assessment.data_quality_note: Simulation-only evaluation with no real-world dataset. For a paper claiming 5G-NR infrastructure compatibility and sub-meter accuracy, the absence of any empirical validation—哪怕是 SDR-based测量——is a critical weakness. The scale and realism of the simulation scenario are also unspecified.
quality_assessment.prediction_reason: The paper targets a relevant practical problem but offers an incrementally novel solution (quality-adaptive exponential smoothing) with no theoretical depth and simulation-only validation. The 3GPP-awareness angle adds some value, but without real data or rigorous analysis, the contribution is thin for a top venue.

overflow_list.title: FORSLICE: An Automated Formal Framework for Efficient PRB-Allocation towards Slicing Multiple Network Services
overflow_list.authors: Debarpita Banerjee, Sumana Ghosh, Snigdha Das, Shilpa Budhkar, Rana Pratap Sircar
overflow_list.abstract:
Network slicing is a modern 5G technology that provides efficient network experience for diverse use cases. It is a technique for partitioning a single physical network infrastructure into multiple virtual networks, called slices, each equipped for specific services and requirements. In this work, we particularly deal with radio access network (RAN) slicing and resource allocation to RAN slices. In 5G, physical resource blocks (PRBs) being the fundamental units of radio resources, our main focus is to allocate PRBs to the slices efficiently. While addressing a spectrum of needs for multiple services or the same services with multi-priorities, we need to ensure two vital system properties: i) fairness to every service type (i.e., providing the required resources and a desired range of throughput) even after prioritizing a particular service type, and ii) PRB-optimality or minimizing the unused PRBs in slices. These serve as the core performance evaluation metrics for PRB-allocation in our work.
  We adopt the 3-layered hierarchical PRB-partitioning technique for allocating PRBs to network slices. The case-specific, AI-based solution of the state-of-the-art method lacks sufficient correctness to ensure consistent system performance. To achieve guaranteed correctness and completeness, we leverage formal methods and propose the first approach for a fair and optimal PRB distribution to RAN slices. We formally model the PRB-allocation problem as a 3-layered framework, FORSLICE, specifically by employing satisfiability modulo theories. Next, we apply formal verification to ensure that the desired system properties: fairness and PRB-optimality, are satisfied by the model. The proposed method offers an efficient, versatile and automated approach compatible with all 3-layered hierarchical network structure configurations, yielding significant system property improvements compared to the baseline.
overflow_list.summary_cn: 针对5G RAN切片的PRB分配问题，提出首个基于形式化方法（可满足性模理论）的公平最优分配框架FORSLICE，确保资源分配的公平性和利用率，为三层分层网络结构提供高效自动化方案。

strengths: First formal-methods-based approach for PRB allocation in RAN slicing — genuine methodological novelty vs. dominant AI/heuristic paradigm
SMT-based modeling provides correctness and completeness guarantees that AI/ML approaches fundamentally cannot offer
Dual property verification (fairness + PRB-optimality) is well-motivated and cleanly formulated
Compatible with all 3-layered hierarchical configurations, offering versatility
limitations: Abstract mentions only a single 'baseline' comparison — no indication of comprehensive benchmarking against state-of-the-art AI/optimization methods
No mention of real-world dataset or large-scale simulation; formal methods papers often evaluate on small synthetic scenarios, raising scalability concerns
SMT solver runtime is unaddressed — PRB allocation in live RAN operates at sub-millisecond timescales; solver overhead may be prohibitive for real-time deployment
The 3-layered hierarchical structure is adopted from prior work; the core allocation logic within each layer lacks detail in the abstract
quality_assessment.data_quality_note: Abstract provides no evidence of real-world or large-scale simulation data. Mentions only 'significant improvements compared to the baseline' without specifying dataset, scale, or baseline identity. Likely relies on small synthetic/formal verification case studies, which is insufficient for validating practical RAN slicing performance claims.
quality_assessment.prediction_reason: The paper's primary contribution is replacing AI-based heuristics with SMT-formalized guarantees — a legitimate and well-motivated methodological shift. However, formal methods approaches in wireless resource allocation consistently struggle with scalability and real-time feasibility, and the abstract gives no indication the authors have addressed this gap. The absence of any mention of real data, simulation scale, or solver runtime performance significantly drags down the data quality and practical impact scores.

overflow_list.title: RieIF: Knowledge-Driven Riemannian Information Flow for Robust Spatio-Temporal Graph Signal Prediction in 6G Wireless Networks
overflow_list.authors: Zhonghao Jiu, Yongming Huang, Fan Meng, Hang Zhan, Zening Liu, Xiaohu You
overflow_list.abstract: With 6G evolving towards intelligent network autonomy, artificial intelligence (AI)-native operations are becoming pivotal. Wireless networks continuously generate rich and heterogeneous data, which inherently exhibits spatio-temporal graph structure. However, limited radio resources result in incomplete and noisy network measurements. This challenge is further intensified when a target variable and its strongest correlates are missing over contiguous intervals, forming systemic blind spots. To tackle this issue, we propose RieIF (Knowledge-driven Riemannian Information Flow), a geometry-consistent framework that incorporates knowledge graphs (KGs) for robust spatio-temporal graph signal prediction. For analytical tractability within the Fisher-Rao geometry, we project the input from a Riemannian manifold onto a positive unit hypersphere, where angular similarity is computationally efficient. This projection is implemented via a graph transformer, using the KG as a structural prior to constrain attention and generate a micro stream. Simultaneously, a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model captures temporal dynamics to produce a macro stream. Finally, the micro stream (highlighting geometric shape) and the macro stream (emphasizing signal strength) are adaptively fused through a geometric gating mechanism for signal recovery. Experiments on three wireless datasets show consistent improvements under systemic blind spots, including up to 31% reduction in root mean squared error and up to 3.2 dB gain in recovery signal-to-noise ratio, while maintaining robustness to graph sparsity and measurement noise.
overflow_list.summary_cn: RieIF：基于知识图谱与黎曼几何的无线网络时空图信号恢复框架。将黎曼流形投影至正定单位超球面，通过图Transformer结合知识先验与LSTM分别提取几何特征与时序动态，实验在系统盲区场景下均方根误差降低31%，恢复信噪比提升3.2dB。

strengths: Strong 6G network AI motivation — tackles a real and timely problem: incomplete/noisy measurements with systemic blind spots
Creative architectural fusion: Fisher-Rao geometry + knowledge graph + graph transformer + LSTM — non-trivial integration that is not just a vanilla GNN baseline
Micro-macro stream decomposition with geometric gating is architecturally distinctive and conceptually sound
Solid performance claims: 31% RMSE reduction and 3.2 dB SNR gain across three datasets — concrete and credible
Addresses graph sparsity and noise robustness explicitly, which are practical concerns in real wireless deployments
limitations: Dataset details entirely absent from abstract — no dataset names, scale, collection methodology, or real-vs-synthetic designation; major red flag for data quality assessment
Baseline comparison is vague ('consistent improvements') — no comparison against state-of-the-art methods or ablation of individual components provided
Mathematical depth is impossible to verify from abstract alone — Fisher-Rao projection details, attention formulation, and gating mechanism need scrutiny
Knowledge graph construction and its integration mechanism are under-described — critical for reproducibility
No complexity/real-time feasibility discussion — 6G deployment requires computational budget analysis, which is missing
Angular similarity on hypersphere for Fisher-Rao geometry is a known approach in some domains (e.g., brain connectomics); novelty may be partially incremental over prior Riemannian deep learning work
quality_assessment.data_quality_note: Three wireless datasets are claimed but not named, characterized (real vs synthetic), or sized. Performance metrics are specific (31% RMSE reduction, 3.2 dB SNR gain) which adds credibility, but without dataset identity or data scale, reproducibility and generalizability cannot be assessed. This is the most significant gap in the submission.
quality_assessment.prediction_reason: Scores are assigned with medium confidence. The paper presents a well-motivated and architecturally novel framework for 6G spatio-temporal prediction, with plausible mathematical scaffolding (Fisher-Rao geometry) and credible performance gains. However, the data quality is unverifiable from the abstract alone (no dataset names or scale), technical rigor details are hidden behind the abstraction, and baseline comparisons are insufficiently described. If the datasets are real and the baselines are truly state-of-the-art (e.g., Graph WaveNet, AGCRN), the score would increase to ~72; if datasets are synthetic or small-scale, it would drop to ~52. The overall 62 reflects a competent but incomplete submission that likely needs major revision before acceptance at a top venue.

overflow_list.title: Enhancing Secure Intent-Based Networking with an Agentic AI: The EU Project MARE Approach
overflow_list.authors: Iulisloi Zacarias, Marla Grunewald, Fin Gentzen, Xavi Masip-Bruin, Admela Jukan
overflow_list.abstract: In the EU project MARE, a novel plane was proposed and used in combination with intent-based networking (IBN), allowing the operator to focus on what, rather than on how. Recently, LLMs have been successfully employed to translate the high-level intents into low-level actions. The open challenge is to understand how IBN can be effectively enhanced with LLM and the emerging agentic AI for security purposes. Enhancing IBN with an agentic AI paradigm introduces significant challenges that existing solutions do not fully address. This paper proposes an enhanced IBN framework with a strong security focus toward agentic AI. We address the architectural and security requirements for a multi-agent intent-based system (IBS) architecture, including a multi-domain IBN. We propose a hierarchical multi-agent and multi-vendor architecture that can also be applied more broadly in 6G architectures and beyond, beyond the security architecture proposed in MARE. The architecture incorporates an interactive intent-processing pipeline using LLMs, and it also allows the IBS to connect to external security knowledge bases, such as MITRE ATT\&CK, MITRE FiGHT, and NIST.
overflow_list.summary_cn: 提出面向安全意图驱动的多智能体增强框架，结合LLM实现高层意图转底层操作，设计跨域多层次架构，支持接入MITRE ATT&CK等安全知识库，为6G安全架构提供新思路。

strengths: Addresses timely intersection of IBN, LLM, and agentic AI for security
Proposes hierarchical multi-agent architecture applicable to 6G
Integrates external security knowledge bases (MITRE ATT&CK, NIST)
EU project backing provides credibility and potential resources
limitations: Abstract lacks any empirical validation or evaluation results
No mention of real testbed, dataset, or prototype implementation
Security claims are architectural only without threat modeling or attack evaluation
Vague on how LLM intent translation handles ambiguity or adversarial inputs
No comparison with existing IBN security solutions
quality_assessment.data_quality_note: Abstract mentions no experiments, datasets, simulations, or even preliminary results. Claims are purely architectural/conceptual. Score 30 reflects complete absence of empirical data—cannot assess validity of security enhancements without evaluation.
quality_assessment.prediction_reason: Position paper proposing architecture without validation. May get workshop acceptance but unlikely for top-tier venue without experimental results. Score 52 reflects conceptual promise undermined by lack of data.

overflow_list.title: Real-World LoRaWAN Performance and Propagation Modeling Using UAV, Helikite, and Vehicle-Based Measurements
overflow_list.authors: Sergio Vargas Villar, Simran Singh, Özgür Özdemir, Mihail L. Sichitiu, İsmail Güvenç
overflow_list.abstract: This paper presents a field-based evaluation of Long Range Wide Area Network (LoRaWAN) signal propagation conducted at two locations within the Aerial Experimentation and Research Platform for Advanced Wireless (AERPAW) testbed: Lake Wheeler Field and NC State University's Centennial Campus. Three distinct transmission platforms were deployed, a ground vehicle, a multirotor drone at 50 meters, and a helikite at a steady altitude of 150 meters and 300 meters approximately. These platforms enabled a comparative study on how altitude, mobility, and terrain influence wireless signal reception across a LoRaWAN gateway network. We analyze received signal strength (RSSI) and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) as functions of distance and spreading factor (SF). Three complementary metrics are visualized: SNR versus distance with demodulation thresholds, probability of successful reception, and SNR boxplots grouped by distance bins. These plots reveal link degradation patterns and demonstrate the role of adaptive SF selection in maintaining communication reliability. To characterize propagation behavior, we apply a log-distance path loss model to empirical data from the ground vehicle experiment, which encompass both rural and urban non-line-of-sight (NLOS) conditions. Model parameters are optimized through error minimization techniques. Our results show that the helikite platform, due to its stable high-altitude position, provided the most reliable and consistent link performance. Conversely, the drone and vehicle exhibited higher variability due to movement, obstructions, and terrain-induced multipath. These findings demonstrate the influence of platform dynamics and altitude on LoRaWAN reception performance, providing support for future aerial network planning efforts.
overflow_list.summary_cn: 基于AERPAW测试平台的LoRaWAN空对地信号传播特性研究。评估了地面车辆、多旋翼无人机（50m）和氦气飞艇（150-300m）三种发射平台对RSSI和SNR的影响，结果表明高海拔稳定平台（氦气飞艇）提供最可靠的链路性能，为空中网络规划提供实测依据。

strengths: Multi-platform comparative study (drone, helikite, ground vehicle) provides useful altitude/dynamics perspective for LoRaWAN — a practical and increasingly relevant scenario
Real field measurements at two distinct AERPAW testbed sites (rural lake + urban campus) covering both LOS and NLOS conditions — adds ecological validity
Analysis of both RSSI and SNR across varying spreading factors is appropriate for LoRaWAN characterization
Three complementary visualization approaches (SNR thresholds, reception probability, boxplots) cover the key angles of link quality assessment
Log-distance path loss model fitted to empirical data with error minimization — standard but sound propagation modeling approach
Results show helikite superiority due to stable altitude, which is a non-obvious and practically useful finding for network planners
limitations: Novelty is limited — multi-platform LoRaWAN propagation measurement studies have appeared in the literature (e.g., similar work exists for drone-to-ground links); the incremental contribution over existing work is unclear
No mention of number of measurement points, number of transmissions, repeatability, or statistical confidence intervals — cannot assess whether the dataset is sufficient to support the conclusions
Log-distance path loss model is the most basic propagation model; more advanced models (e.g., close-in reference, floating intercept, alpha-beta-gamma) are standard in the literature and absent here
Helikite at 'approximately' 150m and 300m suggests altitude may not have been precisely controlled — important for altitude-dependent propagation claims
No discussion of antenna characteristics (type, gain, polarization) — critical variables for reproducible propagation measurements
No mention of environmental conditions (temperature, humidity, foliage state) that affect 868/915 MHz propagation
The paper appears to be evaluation-only; no adaptive SF algorithm is proposed or tested, limiting the actionable value of the SF analysis
Abstract does not report key quantitative results (e.g., path loss exponents, typical SNR/RSSI values, range coverage), making it impossible to assess effect sizes from the abstract alone
Centroid vs. average SNR/RSSI treatment not discussed — important for understanding which metric was used in probability-of-reception calculations
quality_assessment.data_quality_note: The study uses real field measurements (not simulation), which is a significant strength over simulation-only papers. However, the abstract omits critical data quality indicators: number of measurement samples, transmission repetitions, environment descriptions, antenna specs, and statistical confidence bounds. The dataset scale is likely modest given it comes from three physical platforms at two sites — enough to demonstrate feasibility but likely insufficient for high-confidence propagation model parameter estimation. Baseline comparison with established propagation models or prior measurement campaigns is not mentioned, making it hard to gauge whether the fitted model is competitive.
quality_assessment.prediction_reason: Overall score 58 reflects a solid real-world measurement contribution with practical insights for aerial LoRaWAN deployment, but limited by moderate novelty (incremental over existing propagation measurement work), absence of statistical rigor details, and a basic modeling approach. The practical impact score is boosted by the clear relevance to aerial network planning; the data score is real-world but flagged for lack of transparency about sample size and repeatability.

overflow_list.title: Modeling and Analysis of Air-to-Ground Cellular KPIs in a 5G Testbed using Android Smartphones
overflow_list.authors: Simran Singh, Anıl Gürses, Özgür Özdemir, Ram Asokan, Mihail L. Sichitiu, İsmail Güvenç, Rudra Dutta, Magreth Mushi
overflow_list.abstract: The integration of cellular communication with Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) extends the range of command and control and payload communications of autonomous UAV applications. Accurate modeling of this air-to-ground wireless environment aids UAV mission planning. Models built on and insights obtained from real-life experiments intricately capture the variations in air-to-ground link quality with UAV position, offering more fidelity for simulations and system design than those that rely on generic theoretical models designed for ground scenarios or ray-tracing simulations. In this work, we conduct aerial flights at the Aerial Experimentation and Research Platform for Advanced Wireless (AERPAW) Lake Wheeler testbed to study the variation in key performance indicators (KPIs) of a private 4G/5G cellular base station (BS) with the UAV's altitude, distance from the BS, elevation, and azimuth relative to the BS. Variations in 4G and 5G physical layer KPIs and application layer throughput are logged and analyzed, using two Android smartphones: a Keysight Nemo device, with enhanced KPI access, through a rooted operating system, and a standard smartphone running a custom application that utilizes open-source Android APIs. The observed signal strength measurements are compared to theoretical predictions from free space path loss models that incorporate the BS antenna radiation patterns. Mathematical model parameters for polynomial curve approximations are derived to fit the observed data. Light machine learning approaches, namely random forests, gradient boosting regressors and neural networks, are used to model KPI behaviour as a function of UAV position relative to the BS. The insights and models generated from real-life experiments in this study can serve as valuable tools in the design, simulation and deployment of cellular communication-based UAV systems.
overflow_list.summary_cn: 蜂窝通信与无人机的集成扩展了自主无人机应用的指挥控制和载荷通信范围。精确建模这种空对地无线环境有助于无人机任务规划。基于真实实验构建的模型和获得的洞察，能够精细捕捉空对地链路质量随无人机位置的变化，为仿真和系统设计提供比依赖地面场景通用理论模型或射线追踪仿真更高的保真度。本研究在AERPAW Lake Wheeler试验台进行空中飞行实验，研究私有4G/5G蜂窝基站的关键性能指标(KPI)随无人机高度、与基站距离、仰角和方位角的变化。使用两部安卓智能手机记录并分析4G和5G物理层KPI及应用层吞吐量的变化：一部是Keysight Nemo设备，通过root系统增强KPI访问能力；另一部是运行利用开源安卓API的定制应用的标准智能手机。将观测到的信号强度测量值与结合基站天线辐射图型的自由空间路径损耗模型理论预测进行对比。推导多项式曲线逼近的数学模型参数以拟合观测数据。采用轻量级机器学习方法（随机森林、梯度提升回归器和神经网络）建立KPI随无人机相对基站位置变化的模型。本研究真实实验产生的洞察和模型可作为基于蜂窝通信的无人机系统设计、仿真和部署的宝贵工具。

strengths: Real-world aerial flight experiments at a dedicated testbed (AERPAW), not purely simulation-based — significant strength for data quality
Comparison of two Android devices (rooted Keysight Nemo vs. standard smartphone with open-source APIs) adds practical insight into measurement accessibility
Multi-dimensional analysis (altitude, distance, elevation, azimuth) provides a comprehensive spatial characterization of A2G channels
Comparison with theoretical free-space path loss models incorporating BS antenna radiation patterns anchors empirical findings against established theory
Multiple modeling approaches (polynomial curve fitting + ML: RF, GBR, NN) provide flexibility for different use cases
limitations: Novelty is limited — air-to-ground channel measurement campaigns with UAVs have been extensively studied; the incremental contribution is primarily the testbed and device comparison rather than new methodology or fundamental insight
Uses a private 4G/5G BS in a single testbed environment (Lake Wheeler) — generalizability to urban, suburban, or other deployments is uncertain
ML models (RF, GBR, NN) described as 'light' — unclear if they offer meaningful improvement over simpler parametric models; risk of overfitting to a limited flight dataset
Abstract does not clearly articulate what new insights were discovered beyond 'variations exist with position' — the key findings are not highlighted
No mention of dataset size (number of flights, data points), which is critical for assessing the robustness of the derived models
Polynomial curve approximations are a basic fitting technique; without cross-validation or out-of-sample validation details, their utility is questionable
quality_assessment.data_quality_note: The paper uses real-world flight data from a dedicated testbed (AERPAW), which is a genuine strength. Two different measurement devices (rooted and non-rooted Android) add robustness. However, the data comes from a single BS in a single environment, and the abstract does not disclose the scale of the dataset (number of flights, measurement duration, data points). The comparison against free-space path loss with antenna patterns is appropriate but standard. Overall: real data elevates this above purely simulation papers, but limited scope and unknown dataset size prevent a higher score.
quality_assessment.prediction_reason: The paper's main value lies in real-world A2G measurement data from a unique testbed, but novelty is modest — this is fundamentally a measurement campaign paper with standard ML fitting on top. The rigor appears adequate (multi-device, multi-variable, comparison to theory) but unexceptional. Practical impact is moderate: useful for the AERPAW community and cellular-UAV system designers, but single-site results limit generalizability. The abstract is well-structured but fails to highlight concrete findings, slightly hurting presentation.

overflow_list.title: A Family of Open Time-Series Foundation Models for the Radio Access Network
overflow_list.abstract: The Radio Access Network (RAN) is evolving into a programmable and disaggregated infrastructure that increasingly relies on AI-native algorithms for optimization and closed-loop control. However, current RAN intelligence is still largely built from task-specific models tailored to individual functions, resulting in model fragmentation, limited knowledge sharing across tasks, poor generalization, and increased system complexity. To address these limitations, we introduce TimeRAN, a unified multi-task learning framework for time-series modeling in the RAN. TimeRAN leverages a lightweight time-series foundation model with few task-specific heads to learn transferable representations that can be efficiently adapted across diverse tasks with limited supervision. To enable large-scale pretraining, we further curate and open-source TimeRAN DataPile, the largest time-series corpus for RAN analytics to date, comprising over 355K time series and 0.56B measurements across diverse telemetry sources, protocol layers, and deployment scenarios. We evaluate TimeRAN across a comprehensive set of RAN analytics tasks, including anomaly detection, classification, forecasting, and imputation, and show that it achieves state-of-the-art performance with minimal or no task-specific fine-tuning. Finally, we integrate TimeRAN into a proof-of-concept 5G testbed and demonstrate that it operates efficiently with limited resource requirements in real-world scenarios.
overflow_list.summary_cn: 提出TimeRAN统一多任务学习框架，利用轻量化时序基础模型学习可迁移表示，支持异常检测、分类、预测等多任务，并开源迄今最大RAN时序数据集TimeRAN DataPile（355K时序、5.6亿测量点）。

strengths: First time-series foundation model specifically designed for RAN domain
Multi-task learning approach addresses model fragmentation in current RAN AI systems
TimeRAN DataPile is the largest open-source RAN time-series corpus (355K series, 0.56B measurements)
Comprehensive evaluation across four diverse tasks (anomaly detection, classification, forecasting, imputation)
Real-world validation via 5G testbed integration demonstrating practical feasibility
Open-source contribution enables reproducibility and community adoption
limitations: Abstract does not specify baseline comparison details or performance margins
Limited information on model architecture complexity and inference latency
Testbed proof-of-concept may not reflect full-scale deployment challenges
Domain-specific foundation model may have limited transferability beyond RAN
quality_assessment.data_quality_note: Strong data quality score. The TimeRAN DataPile represents a substantial contribution with 355K real-world time series from diverse telemetry sources, protocol layers, and deployment scenarios. Open-sourcing the dataset significantly enhances reproducibility and enables future research. This addresses a critical gap in RAN analytics where real-world datasets are typically proprietary and scarce.
quality_assessment.prediction_reason: This paper makes a compelling contribution to the emerging field of AI-native RAN. The foundation model paradigm applied to RAN time-series is timely and addresses genuine industry pain points (model fragmentation, limited knowledge sharing). The creation and open-sourcing of a large-scale RAN dataset is a significant contribution that will likely catalyze further research. The combination of strong novelty, real-world data, and practical testbed validation suggests high acceptance probability. Score of 82 reflects solid work with clear industry relevance, though technical depth of baselines and scalability claims would need verification in the full paper.
